event: Early hominins inhabit the Malawi Rift, where fossils of Homo rudolfensis (the Uraha jaw) date to around 2.5 million years ago, among the oldest Homo remains known.
Hunter-gatherer peoples, ancestors of the Twa and later Akafula (Batwa), occupy the region, leaving behind rock art and stone tools around Lake Malawi.
Bantu-speaking agriculturalists begin migrating into the region, introducing ironworking, farming, and pottery and gradually displacing earlier foragers.
Iron Age Bantu communities establish settled villages and clans across the highlands and lakeshore, developing matrilineal social structures.
The Maravi (Malawi) peoples consolidate, with the Phiri clan founding the Maravi Empire under a paramount ruler titled the Kalonga near the southwestern shore of Lake Malawi.
The Maravi Empire reaches its height under the Kalonga, controlling trade routes between the interior and the Indian Ocean coast and supplying ivory and iron.
Portuguese trader Gaspar Bocarro travels through Maravi territory, leaving one of the earliest European records of the kingdom.
The Maravi Empire begins to fragment as subordinate chiefs assert independence and Portuguese and Swahili-Arab traders intensify dealings in ivory and slaves.
Swahili-Arab and Yao traders expand the slave trade through the region, channeling captives toward the East African coast at Kilwa and Zanzibar.
Ngoni groups fleeing the Mfecane upheavals in southern Africa migrate northward into Malawi, raiding and reshaping local societies.
Scottish missionary and explorer David Livingstone reaches Lake Malawi (Lake Nyasa), publicizing the region and decrying the devastating Arab-Swahili slave trade.
Scottish Presbyterian missionaries found the Livingstonia mission and, in 1876, the Blantyre mission, establishing schools and churches that shaped modern Malawi.
The African Lakes Company is established by Scottish businessmen to promote legitimate trade and undermine the slave trade in the Lake Nyasa region.
Britain declares a protectorate over the Shire Highlands as it competes with Portugal for control of the territory.
Britain proclaims the British Central Africa Protectorate, with Harry Johnston as first commissioner, formalizing colonial rule over the area.
British forces wage military campaigns against Yao, Swahili, and Ngoni leaders to suppress the slave trade and impose colonial authority.
The protectorate is renamed Nyasaland, and a colonial legislative and executive council structure is introduced.
John Chilembwe leads an armed uprising against colonial labor abuses and forced recruitment for World War I, an early landmark of African nationalism.
The Nyasaland African Congress is founded, becoming the principal vehicle for African political aspirations against colonial rule.
Britain incorporates Nyasaland into the Federation of Rhodesia and Nyasaland, sparking widespread African opposition.
Dr. Hastings Kamuzu Banda returns from abroad to lead the Nyasaland African Congress and galvanize the independence movement.
Colonial authorities declare a state of emergency, banning the Congress and detaining Banda amid mass protests and deaths.
Banda's Malawi Congress Party wins the legislative elections, paving the way toward self-government.
Nyasaland achieves internal self-government with Banda as prime minister, and the Federation is dissolved at year's end.
Nyasaland gains full independence on 6 July as Malawi, with Hastings Banda as prime minister, and joins the United Nations.
The Cabinet Crisis erupts as several ministers challenge Banda's autocratic style and pro-Western foreign policy, leading to their dismissal and exile.
Malawi becomes a republic with Banda as its first president and is declared a one-party state under the Malawi Congress Party.
Hastings Banda is declared President for Life, cementing decades of authoritarian rule.
The capital is officially moved from Zomba to the newly developed city of Lilongwe.
Three cabinet ministers and a member of parliament die in the Mwanza road incident, widely believed to be political assassinations ordered by the regime.
Catholic bishops issue a pastoral letter criticizing the regime, and pro-democracy protests and strikes mount pressure on Banda's government.
Malawians vote overwhelmingly in a referendum to end one-party rule and adopt multiparty democracy.
Bakili Muluzi of the United Democratic Front wins the first multiparty elections, ending Banda's three-decade rule, under a new democratic constitution.
Former president Hastings Kamuzu Banda dies, closing a defining chapter in Malawi's post-independence history.
Bakili Muluzi is re-elected president in the second multiparty general elections.
A severe famine strikes Malawi following crop failures and grain shortages, prompting international relief efforts.
Bingu wa Mutharika is elected president, later breaking with the UDF to found the Democratic Progressive Party.
Mutharika launches a fertilizer and seed subsidy program that boosts maize harvests and turns Malawi into a regional food exporter.
Bingu wa Mutharika wins re-election in a landslide amid strong economic growth.
Nationwide anti-government protests over fuel shortages, currency scarcity, and governance turn deadly as security forces kill demonstrators.
President Bingu wa Mutharika dies in office of a cardiac arrest, and Vice President Joyce Banda succeeds him as Malawi's first female president.
The 'Cashgate' scandal exposes massive looting of government funds, prompting donors to suspend budget support.
Peter Mutharika, brother of the late Bingu, wins the presidential election, defeating incumbent Joyce Banda.
Peter Mutharika is declared winner of a disputed election, triggering opposition challenges and mass protests over alleged vote tampering.
After courts annul the 2019 result, a historic rerun election brings opposition leader Lazarus Chakwera to the presidency.
Tropical Cyclone Freddy devastates southern Malawi, killing over a thousand people and displacing hundreds of thousands.
Vice President Saulos Chilima and others are killed in a military plane crash, a national tragedy amid ongoing political and economic challenges.
